For more than two decades I served as a United Methodist chaplain under endorsement. I came to understand chaplaincy as a ministry lived at the edge of the Church’s institutional life—present where the Church cannot organize itself in familiar ways, yet still unmistakably the Church’s own.

What I did not fully understand at the outset was how that ministry remains connected to the Church that sends it. That connection is not automatic. It is sustained through relationships, responsibilities, and a set of practices that are often assumed rather than examined.

Over time, I came to think of that connection as a kind of hinge.
